checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the source document and the output JSON. Each block of text (the farm name, address, registry information, reference numbers, coordinates, and base map details) appears exactly once in the JSON output, mirroring the source document. There are no instances of duplicated paragraphs or repeated sections.

The output JSON uses level 2 headings for 'Evergreen Farm' and 'Latitude/Longitude'. This is a flat but valid hierarchy for a single-page document. There are no skipped heading levels (e.g., H1 to H3). The 'Base Map:' section is treated as part of a paragraph block, which is acceptable as it functions as a label in the source text.
The source document contains no tables. The JSON output correctly reflects this by having a table count of 0.

The reading order in the JSON follows the visual hierarchy of the document from top to bottom on the left sidebar, followed by the image (map) which takes up the right/central portion of the page. This logical flow (Evergreen Farm -> Address -> National Register info -> Coordinates list -> Base Map info -> Map image) faithfully represents the intended reading order of the source.

The latitude and longitude coordinates are presented as a numbered list in the source. The JSON output converts this into a 'list' element. Although categorized as 'unordered', it preserves the source numbering ('1) ', '2) ', etc.) within the text content of each list item, effectively preserving the structural meaning and sequence of the list.
The source document contains no URLs or links. The JSON output correctly does not include any injected or fabricated links.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output converts the side-by-side layout (text on left, map on right) of the source PDF into a single-column vertical layout. This is a standard and acceptable transformation for responsive HTML and does not confuse the meaning or visual flow of the information. The information hierarchy remains intact.
The document does not contain any tables. The list of coordinates is correctly rendered as a bulleted list.

The source document is a 'Location Map', where the topographic map is the most critical piece of content. In the source PDF, the map is a large, square-proportioned image showing a significant geographic area including landmarks like 'Muddy Creek' and 'Clemmons'. In the HTML output, the map image is severely truncated, appearing only as a thin horizontal strip. This strip shows only the top edge of the original map ('Jonestown' area), omitting roughly 80% of the map content and rendering the 'Location Map' largely useless for its intended purpose. The 'DRAFT' watermark is also removed, which is an expected accessibility improvement.
checks.b_evidence_source: Topographic map image showing geographic area from 'Jonestown' at the top to 'Clemmons' at the bottom.
checks.b_evidence_output: The map image is rendered as a narrow horizontal slice, showing only the 'Jonestown' label and omitting the vast majority of the geographic features seen in the source.
checks.b_evidence_location: Map image following the 'Base Map' text block

checks.a_analysis: There is no visual duplication of body content. The appearance of 'Evergreen Farm' in the document title and then as a heading is standard accessible document structure and is not a defect.
